title: Bell's Palsy | MedlinePlus
content: Bell's Palsy On this page Basics Summary Start Here Diagnosis and Tests Learn More No links available See, Play and Learn No links available Research Clinical Trials Journal Articles Resources Find an Expert For You Children Patient Handouts Summary Bell's palsy is the most common cause of facial paralysis. It usually affects just one side of the face. Symptoms appear suddenly and are at their worst about 48 hours after they start. They can range from mild to severe and include: Twitching Weakness Paralysis Drooping eyelid or corner of mouth Drooling Dry eye or mouth Excessive tearing in the eye Impaired ability to taste Scientists think that a viral infection makes the facial nerve swell or become inflamed. You are most likely to get Bell's palsy if you are pregnant, diabetic or sick with a cold or flu. Three out of four patients improve without treatment. With or without treatment, most people begin to get better within 2 weeks and recover completely within 3 to 6 months.
